evals.prompt: I'm packaging a Go CLI tool (github.com/example/mytool v1.2.0) with buildGoModule. The tool only needs the binary from cmd/mytool, not the other binaries in the repo. I set vendorHash to lib.fakeHash to bootstrap the hash — is that correct? What's the right workflow, and what should I watch out for?
evals.expected_output: Use lib.fakeHash as a bootstrap sentinel: build once to get the hash mismatch error that prints the correct hash, then replace lib.fakeHash with that hash. Set subPackages = [ "cmd/mytool" ] to build only the target binary. Never commit lib.fakeHash or an empty string as the final vendorHash — both always fail the build. Use vendorHash = null only when the module has no external dependencies.
expectations: Explains lib.fakeHash as a bootstrap sentinel that intentionally fails to reveal the correct hash, not a placeholder to leave in place
Warns explicitly that committing lib.fakeHash or vendorHash = "" as final values will always fail the build
Shows subPackages = [ "cmd/mytool" ] to limit which binaries are built
Mentions vendorHash = null as the correct value only when there are no external dependencies
Avoids suggesting manual hash computation or any workflow other than the fakeHash error-output bootstrap

evals.prompt: I'm writing a stdenv.mkDerivation for a C project. The source has hardcoded paths like /usr/bin/curl and /usr/local/share/myapp. I need to patch them to use Nix store paths. I also need to override buildPhase to run a custom make target. Show me the correct approach for both.
evals.expected_output: Use substituteInPlace with --replace-fail (not --replace) inside postPatch to fix hardcoded paths; ${curl} is Nix interpolation expanding to the store path at instantiation, while $out is a shell variable available at build time. When overriding buildPhase entirely, include runHook preBuild at the start and runHook postBuild at the end — omitting these silently breaks any pre/postBuild hooks set by dependencies.
expectations: Uses --replace-fail instead of --replace, and explains that --replace silently succeeds even when the pattern is absent, leaving the hardcoded path in the binary
Correctly distinguishes ${curl} (Nix interpolation, expands at instantiation) from $out (shell variable, available at build time) inside ''...'' strings
Shows runHook preBuild and runHook postBuild inside the overridden buildPhase
Explains that omitting runHook calls silently breaks setup hooks from dependencies, which is the most common cause of mysterious build failures after adding a dependency
Prefers postPatch for substituteInPlace rather than patchPhase override, to compose with other patches

evals.prompt: I packaged a shell script with stdenv.mkDerivation. The script calls curl and jq at runtime. After building, I ran nix path-info -Sh and the closure is much larger than expected. How do I make curl and jq available to the script at runtime, and how do I diagnose what's actually pulling in the large closure?
evals.expected_output: Use wrapProgram with --prefix PATH : ${lib.makeBinPath [ curl jq ]} in postInstall, with makeWrapper in nativeBuildInputs. To diagnose the large closure, check whether the installed binary is actually a wrapper script (file /nix/store/xxx/bin/myscript will say 'POSIX shell script'), then use strings on it to see embedded store paths, and nix why-depends to trace which dependency is responsible for the size.
expectations: Places makeWrapper in nativeBuildInputs (not buildInputs), because it is a build tool that runs during the build, not a linked library
Uses lib.makeBinPath to construct the PATH prefix rather than manually concatenating store paths
Explains that wrapProgram embeds store paths in a wrapper script, and that every tool in --prefix PATH becomes a runtime closure entry — this is the most common source of unexpected closure size
Shows the diagnostic sequence: file to detect wrapper scripts, strings | grep /nix/store/ to see embedded paths, nix why-depends to trace the dependency chain
Avoids suggesting propagatedBuildInputs or buildInputs for runtime tools that are only shelled out to
